                                  iv
                                                   PREFACE

                                          Eleventh Edition - 2023

The Eleventh Edition of the TEMA Standards was prepared by the Technical Committee of the Tubular Exchanger
Manufacturers Association. Sections have been added to provide direction concerning the design of one pass
floating heads and Type-D high pressure channel closures. The Eleventh Edition introduces an Exchanger Type
Selection Guide which discusses the benefits and disadvantages of various types of exchangers and a section on
Inspection which has information concerning nondestructive examination techniques and recommendations for
their use for shell and tube heat exchangers. This edition includes new appendices for Repairs and Alterations of
Heat Exchangers; for Clad and Overlay Construction; and for Installation, Operation, and Maintenance of Shell and
Tube Heat Exchangers. TEMA has modified the Heat Exchanger Specification Sheet to better define the design
requirements necessary to specify a heat exchanger. This Standard has been revised to include weld calculations
for vertical support lugs, a clarification to the mitigation of erosion of the tube ends due to certain tube side flows,
and a more detailed discussion concerning tube to tubesheet joints.

One of the main goals of the Eleventh Edition is to better present the TEMA Standards to all users. To do this,
content has been expanded to include separate tables and graphics with metric units for more clarity and to provide
units that make practical sense for manufacturers. The editor acknowledges the contributions of Raj Chhatrapati, M.
Venkatesh, Sarath Kumar, and Parikshit Padture in this effort. Their insight and help are very much appreciated.

The editor acknowledges with appreciation the many years of service and contributions of Sam Davis and
Cris Smelley to the TEMA Technical Committee.

Miles Duvall, Editor

                                                    vii
                                          NOTES TO USERS OF
                                         THE TEMA STANDARDS



Three classes of Mechanical Standards, R, C, and B, reflecting acceptable designs for various service applications,
are presented. The user should refer to the definition of each class and choose the one that best fits the specific
need.

Corresponding subject matter in the three classes of Mechanical Standards is covered by paragraphs identically
numbered except for the class prefix letter. Paragraph numbers preceded by RGB indicates that all three classes
are identical. Any reference to a specific paragraph must be preceded by the class designation.

The Recommended Good Practice section has been prepared to assist the designer in areas outside the scope of
the basic Standards. Paragraphs in the Standards having additional information in the RGP section are marked with
an asterisk (*). The reference paragraph in the RGP section has the identical paragraph number, but with an "RGP11
prefix.

It is the intention of the Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association that this edition of its Standards may be used
beginning with the date of issuance, and that its requirements supersede those of the previous edition six months
from such date of issuance, except for heat exchangers contracted for prior to the end of the six month period. For
this purpose, the date of issuance is July 1, 2024.

This Standard has both US Customary (USC) and SI units. A single system of units shall be used for all aspects of
design and fabrication except where otherwise permitted by this Standard, Where not specified, and unless
otherwise indicated, dimensions in USC tables are in inches, and dimensions in SI tables are in millimeters. When
presented together in tables, figures, and text, SI values are presented in parentheses following the USC values
and do not always indicate direct conversions. Tables with an M suffix use SI units and immediately follow the
corresponding USC table; they are not referenced separately in text. Examples of this methodology may be found in
Appendix GG of the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Gode, Section VIII

Questions by registered users on interpretation of the TEMA Standards should be submitted online at
www.tema.org. Questions requiring development of new or revised technical information will only be answered
through an addendum or a new edition of the Standards.

Upon agreement between purchaserand fabricator, exceptions to TEMA requirements are acceptable. An
exchanger may still be considered as meeting TEMA requirements as long as the exception is documented.

www.tema.org                         HEAT EXCHANGER NOMENCLATURE                                              SECTION 1

N-1 SIZE NUMBERING AND TYPE DESIGNATION - RECOMMENDED PRACTICE
     It is recommended that heat exchanger size and type be designated by numbers and letters as described below.
       N-1.1 SIZE
            Sizes of shells (and tube bundles) shall be designated by numbers describing shell (and tube bundle)
            diameters and tube lengths, as follows:
            Nd.1,1 NOMINAL DIAMETER
                   The nominal diameter shall be the inside diameter of the shell in inches (mm), rounded to the
                   nearest inch (10 mm). For kettle reboilers the nominal diameter shall be the port diameter followed
                   by the shell diameter, rounded to the nearest inch (10 mm).
            N-1.1.2 NOMINAL LENGTH
                    The nominal length shall be the tube length in inches (mm). Tube length for straight tubes shall be
                    taken as the actual overall length. For U-tubes the length shall be taken as the approximate straight
                    length from end of tube to bend tangent.
       N-1.2TYPE
            Figure N-1.2 shows some available exchanger component types. Type designation for complete
            assemblies shall be by letters describing each of the front-end stationary, shell, and rear-end types. Type
            designations shall also be used for partial heat exchanger assemblies.
       N-1.3 TYPICAL EXAMPLES
            N-1. 3.1
                       Split- ring floating head exchanger with removable channel and cover, single pass shell, 23 1/4"
                       (591 mm) inside diameter with tubes 16r (4877 mm) long. SIZE 23-192 (590-4877) TYPE AES.
            N-1.3.2
                       U-tube exchanger with bonnet type stationary head, split flow shell, 19" (483 mm) inside diameter
                       with tubes 7' (2134 mm) straight length. SIZE 19-34 (430-2134) TYPE BGU.
            N-1. 3.3
                       Pull-through floating head kettle type reboiler having stationary head integral with tubesheet, 23"
                       (584 mm) port diameter and 37" (940 mm) inside shell diameter with tubes 16d (4877 mm) long.
                       SIZE 23/37-192 (580/940-4877) TYPE CKT.
            N-1. 3.4
                       Fixed tubesheet exchanger with removable channel and cover, bonnet type rear head, two pass
                       shell, 33 1/8" (841 mm) inside diameter with tubes 81 (2438 mm) long SIZE 33-96 (840-2438)
                       TYPE AFM
            N-1,3, 5
                       Fixed tubesheet exchanger having stationary and rear heads integral with tubesheets, single pass
                       shell. 17" (432 mm) inside diameter with tubes 16H (4S77 mm) long. SIZE 17-192 (430-4&77) TYPE
                       NEN.
       N-1.4 SPECIAL DESIGNS
            Special designs are not covered and may be described as best suits the manufacturer. For example, a
            single tube pass, fixed tubesheet exchanger with conical heads may be described as "TYPE BEM with
            Conical Heads". A pull-through floating head exchanger with an integral shell cover may be described as
            "TYPE AET with Integral Shell Cover.




Eleventh Edition                 ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                          1-1
www.tema.org                      HEAT EXCHANGER NOMENCLATURE                                                SECTION 1

       N-1.5 EXCHANGER TYPE SELECTION GUIDE
            Many different configurations of shell and tube heat exchangers are available. Each type has advantages
            and disadvantages, depending on such factors as process and thermal requirements, available space,
            financial budget fouling and cleaning considerations, etc. This section provides information on several of
            the most widely used configurations and briefly discusses some of the issues to consider when planning for
            and selecting a heat ex ch anger configuration,
            N-1. 5.1 STRAIGHT TUBE, FIXED TUBESHEET TYPE HEAT EXCHANGERS
                     Straight tube, fixed tubesheet types are generally the least expensive shell and tube exchanger
                     designs. Examples include BEM, AEL, and NEN types. Advantages include lowcost and access to
                     the tube side for cleaning. However, because the tube bundle is not removable, the shell side
                     cannot be mechanically cleaned. This could be an issue if the shell side fluid causes fouling or
                     sediment buildup. Another disadvantage is that due to the fixed tubesheets, differential thermal
                     expansion between the tubes and shell causes stress in those components. An expansion joint
                     may be required to relieve this stress, adding cost and a potential failure point to the unit.
                     Since NEN and AEL types have bolted end covers, they have the advantage of providing access to
                     the tubes for cleaning without removal of the bonnets or disconnecting inlet and outlet piping. This
                     can save time if frequent cleaning of the tube side is required.
            N-1. 5.2 EXPANSION JOINT CONSIDERATIONS
                    In any shell and tube heat exchanger, operating fluid temperatures result in differing metal
                    temperature for the shell and tubes, which will vary along the length of the unit. While the tube
                    material is in contact with fluids on both sides of the exchanger, the shell material is only in contact
                    with the shell side fluid. Because the shell and tubes reach different temperatures while operating
                    (and may be of different metallurgies), the thermal growth of these components will be different. In
                    any fixed tubesheet type exchanger, this differential thermal expansion between the shell and tubes
                    causes stresses in the shell, tubes, and tubesheets that must be considered by the designer. An
                    expansion joint may be required to relieve this stress. This is often the case when there is a large
                    difference in the operating temperatures of the shell and tube sides For this reason, it is important
                    for the designer to know the expected operating temperatures of the fluids on each side for any
                    expected operating case, even temporary cases (see E-3). Examples of operating cases that
                    should be considered are start-up, shutdown, steady-state operation, external piping loads, and any
                    special events such as upset conditions or steam cleaning operations. An existing fixed-tubesheet
                    exchanger should be evaluated before placing it into any new thermal operating condition.
                    There are two main types of expansion joints: flexible shell elements (FSE) and bellows. The FSE
                    type is a thick-wall expansion joint design (see RCB-8). Although it is less flexible than a thin-wall
                    bellows type, it is much more durable and unlikely to fail, Multiple FSEs can be connected in series
                    to relieve additional thermal stress and allow more expansion.
                    Straight tube, fixed tubesheet type exchangers with larger amounts of expected differential thermal
                    expansion often require a bellows type expansion joint This type is thinner, with one or more
                    convolutions that allow expansion. Due to the thin wall, any minor mechanical damage can cause a
                    leak that may be expensive or difficult to repair Bellows-type joints are usually supplied with a
                    protective shroud to prevent accidental damage.
            N-1, 5.3 REMOVABLE U-TUBE BUNDLE TYPES
                    Types BEU, BKU, and AEU are examples of heat exchangers with removable U-tube bundles.
                    These configurations are economical and allow better access to the shell side for inspection and
                    cleaning than fixed type exchangers. Another advantage is that U-tube configurations allow the
                    tube bundle to freely expand and contract with temperature differences without causing additional
                    stress in the shell. Individual tubes or part of the bundle can expand at different rates without any
                    detrimental effects. Expansion joints are not needed in these types. Another advantage is that the
                    tube bundles can easily be replaced if necessary.
                    A disadvantage of U-tube bundles is the difficulty of cleaning the inside of the bent portion of the
                    tubes. Also, individual tubes inside the bundle cannot be replaced if they become damaged.




Eleventh Edition              ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                           1-2
www.tema.org                     HEAT EXCHANGER NOMENCLATURE                                                SECTION 1

            N-1.5.4 REMOVABLE BUNDLE, FLOATING TUBESHEET TYPES
                   Exchangers with rear end types P, S, T, and W all feature a floating tubesheet (see RGB-5), The
                   floating tubesheet allows bundle thermal expansion and bundle removal like a U-type but maintains
                   the advantages of having straight lubes. Expansion joints are not usually needed with floating
                   tubesheets since the bundle can freely expand and contract with thermal changes, except in one-
                   pass S and T types (see RCB-5,4), Because both shell and tube sides can be accessed more
                   easily for cleaning purposes, floating tubesheet designs are commonly used in dirty services such
                   as refinery applications. Bundle replacements are also easily performed. However, while each of
                   the floating types offers a slightly different design, any floating tubesheet design will be generally
                   more costly to fabricate than the fixed tubesheet or U-tube options P and W type exchangers have
                   the disadvantage that they require a packing seal, which can lead to reliability problems, as these
                   seals can be less robust than gasket type seals; while S and T types include a submerged bolted
                   connection that introduces the potential for shell side and tube side fluids to cross-contaminate if a
                   gasket leak were to occur.
            N-1.5.5 DOUBLE TUBESHEET DESIGNS
                   Most shell and tube configurations can be built with a double tubesheet (see RCB-7.1.2). This
                   design adds a secondary tubesheet and seal to further separate the shell and tube sides of the
                   exchanger. Any leaks at the primary tube-to-tubesheet joint are contained in a secondary chamber
                   or vented before reaching the other side. This option can add significant cost to the unit price.
                   However, double tubesheets minimize the risk of cross-contamination between shell and tube sides
                   due to a failure at the tube to tubesheet joint. When a leak from one side to the other must be
                   avoided for safety or process reasons, double tubesheets should be considered.
            N-1.5.6 SHELL CONFIGURATIONS
                   The simplest, most common, and usually least expensive shell type is TENA E' type, in which the
                   shell side fluid flows in a single pass from one end to the other. The F' type uses a longitudinal
                   baffle to create a fwo-pass shell, which can be used when the exchanger has a temperature cross
                   (the cold stream leaves at a higher temperature than the outlet of the hot stream). Longitudinal
                   baffles can be welded to the shell or installed in the bundle with a flexible seal at the shell. G' and
                    H1 type shells use central baffles to split the shell side flow and are often used for thermosyphon
                   reboilers. Kettle shells (type 'K) include a larger diameter shell to create a vapor space above the
                   tube bundle and are also used for reboilers. Types 'J’ and 'X' provide other options for divided and
                   cross flow on the shell side.

N-3 DEFINITIONS
 1.    Baffle is a device to direct the shell side fluid across the tubes for optimum heat transfer,
 2.    Double Tubesheet Construction is a type of construction in which two (2) spaced tubesheets or equivalent are
       employed in lieu Of the single tubesheet at one or both ends Of the heat exchanger.
 3.    Effective Shell and Tube Side Design Pressures are the resultant load values expressed as uniform pressures
       used in the determination of tubesheet thickness for fixed tubesheet heat exchangers and are functions of the
       shell side design pressure, the tube side design pressure, the equivalent differential expansion pressure and the
       equivalent bolting pressure.
 4.    Equivalent Bolting Pressure is the pressure equivalent resulting from the effects of bolting loads imposed on
       tubesheets in a fixed tubesheet heat exchanger when the tubesheets ane extended for bolting as flanged
       connections.
 5     Equivalent Differential Expansion Pressure is the pressure equivalent resulting from the effect of tubesheet
       loadings in a fixed tubesheet heat exchanger imposed by the restraint of differential thermal expansion between
       shell and tubes.
 6.    Expanded Tube Joint I s the tube-to-tubesheet join t achI eved by mec hani cal, hydra uIio, or explosi ve expan sio n of
       the tube into the tube hole in the tubesheet.
 7.    Expansion Joint "J" Factor is the ratio of the spring rate of the expansion joint to the sum of the axial spring rate of
       the shell and the spring rate of the expansion joint. Refer to section A.1.5.1
 8     Flange Load Concentration Factors are factors used to compensate for the uneven application of bolting
       moments due to large bolt spacing.
 9     Minimum and Maximum Baffle and Support Spacings are design limitations for the spacing of baffles to provide
       for mechanical integrity and thermal and hydraulic effectiveness of the bundle. The possibility for induced
       vibration has not been considered in establishing these values.
10.    Normal Operating Conditions of a shell and tube heat exchanger are the thermal and hydraulic performance
       requirements generally specified for sizing the heat exchanger.
11.    Pulsating Fluid Conditions are conditions of flow generally characterized by rapid fluctuations in pressure and flow
       rate resulting from sources outside Of the heat exchanger.
12     Seismic Loadings are forces and moments resulting in induced stresses on any member of a heat exchanger due
       to pulse mode or complex waveform accelerations to the heat exchanger, such as those resulting from
       earthquakes.
13.    Shell and Tube Mean Metal Temperatures are the average metal temperatures through the shell and tube
       thicknesses integrated over the length of the heat exchanger for a given steady state operating condition.
14.    Shut-Down is the condition of operation which exists from the time of steady state operating conditions to the time
       that flow of both process streams has ceased.
15.    Start-Up is the condition of operation which exists from the time that flow of either or both process streams is
       initiated to the time that steady state operating conditions are achieved.
16     Support plate is a device to support the bundle or to reduce unsupported tube span without consideration for heat
       transfer.
17     Tubesheet Ligament is the shortest distance between edge of adjacent tube holes in the tube pattern.
18.    Welded Tube Joint is a tube-to-tubesheet joint where the tube is welded to the tubesheet.




Eleventh Edition                ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                          1-8
www.tema.org               HEAT EXCHANGER FABRICATION TOLERANCES                                       SECTION 2

F-1 EXTERNAL DIMENSIONS, NOZZLE AND SUPPORT LOCATIONS
     Standard tolerances for process flow nozzles and support locations and projections are shown in
     Figure F-1.

www.tema.org        GENERAL FABRICATION AND PERFORMANCE INFORMATION                                          SECTION 3

G-1 HEAT EXCHANGER SPECIFICATION SHEETS
     The TEMA Heat Exchanger Specification Sheets in Figure G-1 are provided so that the purchaser or end user can
     clearly define and communicate the data necessary to design and fabricate a shell and tube heat exchanger. All
     design requirements and process information (if known) should be completed by the purchaser. Supplemental
     documents or data sheets may be used in addition to or instead of these specification sheets The designer or
     manufacturer may also use these specification sheets to report results of thermal or mechanical exchanger designs
     to the purchaser. The specification sheets are available on the TEMA website www.tema.org.
     In fixed tubesheet exchangers, thermal expansion differences between the shell and tubes must be considered by
     the designer to determine the need for an expansion joint. The purchaser shall specify all expected thermal
     conditions for the exchanger in the data sheet section "Design, Operating, and Upset Conditions.’' In addition to
     standard operating cases, these cases may include cleanout, loss of flow on one side, or other upset conditions
     (see E-3). If mean metal temperatures are not known, inlet and outlet fluid temperatures should be specified If the
     exchanger is operated under thermal conditions outside of those specified, damage to the exchanger can occur
     (see G-5.2).
G-2 INSPECTION AND SHOP OPERATION
       G-2.1 MANUFACTURER’S INSPECTION
             Inspection and testing of units will be provided by the manufacturer unless otherwise specified. The
             manufacturer shall carry out the inspections required by the Code, customer specifications, and those
             required by state and local codes when the purchaser specifies the plant location,
       G-2.2 PURCHASER'S INSPECTION
             The purchaser shall have the right to make inspections during fabrication and to witness any tests when so
             requested. Advance notification shall be given as agreed between the manufacturer and the purchaser.
             Inspection by the purchaser does not relieve the manufacturer of any contractual responsibilities. Any
             additional tests required by the purchaser, above those already agreed to, will be to the purchaser's
             account. Cost for remedial work as a result of these additional tests will also be to the purchaser's account.
       G-2.3 NON-DESTRUCTIVE TESTING TECHNIQUES
             A variety of inspection methods can be performed on pressure equipment without causing any damage to
             the equipment. These inspection techniques are collectively known as non -destructive testing or non¬
             destructive examination (NDT or NDE). This section provides a brief overview of several testing methods,
             as well as TEMA' s recommendations for their use in exchanger fabrication,
             G-2.3.1 RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING (RT)
                     This testing method is used to detect discontinuities such as porosity, cracks and inclusions in
                     materials or welds. Radiographic testing (radiography) is accomplished by placing film or a digital
                     detector on one side of a weld, and introducing a radiographic source, either an X-ray tube or a
                     radioactive isotope, on the other side of the weld. Any radiation from the source that is not
                     absorbed by the weld passes through and exposes the film (or detector), This leaves an image of
                     the internal structure Of the weld once the film is developed. The amount of energy absorbed by the
                     weld depends upon its thickness and density. Cracks and porosity will appear as dark indications
                     on the film. Low density inclusions will also appear as dark areas, while high density inclusions will
                     appear as light areas.
                     The amount of radiography required is dependent upon the Code and end user specifications.
                     Increasing the level of radiography will increase the efficiency factor used in the calculations, which
                     determine the required thickness of the various components of the exchanger. This will increase
                     the cost of the NDE but can decrease the material and labor costs, allowing the designer some
                     flexibility. An experienced designer might consider different levels of radiography with respect to
                     Code, contractual specifications, their shop practices, metallurgy, material availability, and overall
                     cost of the equipment.
                     An advantage of this testing method is that it is a positive method for detecting porosity, inclusions,
                     cracks, and voids in materials and welds. It can also provide a permanent record of the quality of
                     the weld. Since this is a volumetric testing method, sub-surface weld defects can be found with
                     radiography

www.tema.org       GENERAL FABRICATION AND PERFORMANCE INFORMATION                                          SECTION 3


                    A disadvantage of this method is that it generally requires access to bath sides of the area to be
                    examined- Radiography does not lend itself to inspection Of some weld geometries Such as the
                    corner joints of nozzle attachment welds or certain cylinder to tubesheet welds. Radiography is also
                    a relatively slow and expensive method. There are also inherent safety considerations with this
                    method since it uses radioactive sources.
                    With specialized equipment, it is possible to radiograph tube-to-tubesheet welds. This technique is
                    expensive and should only be used on the most critical tube end joints.
                    TELIA Recommendations:
                    A minimum of spot radiography should be performed on heat exchangers. Additional RT may be
                    dictated by Code or contractual requirements.
            G-2.3.2 ULTRASONIC TESTING (UT)
                    This method uses high frequency sound waves to evaluate materials and welds A transducer is
                    used which contains a crystal that vibrates when excited by high frequency voltage. The ultrasonic
                    vibrations from the transducer are transmitted to the piece being tested through a coupling fluid
                    (couplant) The ultrasonic waves travel through the test piece until they are reflected by either a
                    boundary edge or by a discontinuity. The returning echoes are detected by the transducer and
                    displayed on a screen indicating the time (distance) and amplitude of the signal. Since the velocity
                    of the wave through the material is nearly constant, it is passible to determine the nature (detection,
                    location, size and shape) of the discontinuity.
                    There are three types of ultrasonic waves: longitudinal (compression), shear (transverse), and
                    surface (Rayleigh). Longitudinal waves travel the fastest with greater penetrating ability, but less
                    resolution due to the longer wavelength. Thickness measurements and straight beam (0D)
                    inspections use longitudinal waves. Shear waves are created by introducing the longitudinal wave
                    at an angle to the surface of the part. For inspection purposes, the angle is chosen so the
                    longitudinal wave is completely reflected, leaving an angled (refracted) shear wave inside the part.
                    The speed is about half that of longitudinal waves, resulting in a shorter wavelength, thus the
                    resolution and detectability of smaller discontinuities is improved- Shear waves are commonly used
                    for weld inspection. Surface waves are less frequently used but can detect breaks in the surface
                    such as cracks.
                    Traditional UT normally uses a single transducer that both sends and receives energy (known as
                    pulse-echo) and is most often applied directly to the part. However, advances in technology have
                    led to increased use of Phased Array Ultrasonic Testing (PAUT). PAUT uses multiple transducer
                    elements contained within a single probe block A computer controls the direction and focus of the
                    sound and can scan larger sections of material at once. This method is more versatile and more
                    accurate than traditional techniques. Data can be recorded and stored as a permanent record.
                    These principals have been further expanded to produce high resolution and highly sensitive
                    inspection techniques like Full Matrix Capture (FLIC), also known as Total Focus Method (TFM).
                    However, these techniques require greater computing power as well as increased data storage.
                    Other multi- pro be techniques utilizing through transmission (pitch-catch) such as Time of Flight
                    Diffraction (TOFD) are used to increase efficiency and detectability. TOFD is often used in tandem
                    with phased array for fast and accurate flaw sizing.
                    Ultrasonic testing offers several benefits in the construction of heat exchangers. Under certain
                    conditions, the Code allows ultrasonic testing in lieu of radiography, Compared to radiography,
                    ultrasonic testing offers similar full volumetric analysis of welds, but is safer and can be performed
                    nearby other personnel. Ultrasonic testing can be performed on joints where radiography may not
                    be possible, such as nozzle attachment welds and certain tubesheet-to-cylinder welds of fixed
                    tubesheet exchangers. Closing seams that cannot be readily radiographed due to limited access for
                    either the source or film are also good candidates for ultrasonic examination-
                    Ultrasonic testing can also be used to determine the as-built or baseline thickness of various
                    components of a heat exchanger, which can be compared to future thickness measurements to
                    determine erosion/corrosion rales and expected remaining life.
                    Ultrasonic flaw detection requires a highly skilled technician and can be limited by part surface
                    conditions, temperatures, and geometry. Testing results are more subjective than other methods
                    such as radiography.
                    TEMA Recommendations:


Eleventh Edition              ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                       3-6
www.tema.org       GENERAL FABRICATION AND PERFORMANCE INFORMATION                                          SECTION 3

                    When specified by the purchaser, baseline ultrasonic thickness measurements shall be recorded
                    for all shells, channels, heads, and nozzle cylinders. On shells and channels, measurements
                    should be taken at the four cardinal points at each cylinder end and at every 60" (1 524 mm) of
                    length For heads, measurements should be taken at four cardinal points, 6" (1 52.4 mm) from the
                    center and in the knuckle area. Nozzle cylinders 4 NFS (DN 100) and greater should have one set
                    of readings at the four cardinal points. Nozzles less than 4" NFS (DN 1 00) should have two
                    readings recorded, 1 60° apart. All measurements should be provided to the end user in a report
                    with test locations identified.
            G-2.3.3 HARDNESS TESTING
                    Hardness testing is the determination of the resistance of a material to permanent deformation by
                    the penetration of a harder material. This method can be used to measure the hardness of either
                    base materials, welds, and/or heat affected zones around welds. This is useful in order to evaluate
                    properties such as strength, ductility and wear resistance. Hardness can be an indicator of the
                    effectiveness of material heat treatment and workability. It can also reveal susceptibility issues,
                    including cracking, due to exposure to environmental factors. When performing a hardness test, a
                    specifically dimensioned and loaded indenter is pressed into the surface of the material or weld
                    being tested. The shape, size and load of the indenter is specific to the hardness test method being
                    employed. The relative hardness is determined by measuring either the depth or the size of ths
                    impression left by the indenter. Typical hardness test methods are Rockwell (HRB or HRC), Vickers
                    (HV), and Brinell (HB), among others.
                    Hardness testing is a relatively simple and inexpensive n on-destructive test that can be performed
                    on the surfaces of materials or welds. However, it does not find weld or base metal defects, and
                    hardness information is of limited value for many common construction materials.
                    TENA Recommendations:
                    TENA does not recommend hardness testing unless otherwise specified by Code or contractual
                    requirements.
            G-2.3.4 MAGNETIC PARTICLE TESTING (MT)
                    This method is used to detect cracks or other indications at or near the surface of welds or base
                    metals that would otherwise not be seen by visual inspection. In the most commonly used type of
                    MT, a magnetic field is first applied to the part with a hand-held probe or yoke. The surface Of the
                    part is then covered with small iron particles in either a powder form or suspended in a solution.
                    Any crack in the material will cause a disruption in the magnetic field, which will draw the particles
                    to it and make it plainly visible. The dry powder technique is more suitable to be used outdoors or in
                    brightly lit areas, to inspect less accessible parts, or to inspect high temperature parts. A technique
                    using fine fluorescent particles suspended in a solution (wet fluorescent magnetic particle or
                    WFMT) can increase detectability of smaller indications. The fluorescent particles glow when
                    viewed under an ultraviolet light The inspection area must be dark or shielded from bright light for
                    this technique. The part surface must also be free of grease or other detergents that may be
                    fluorescent.
                    Since the magnetic particle testing method uses magnetism, it cannot be performed on non¬
                    magnetic materials such as stainless steel, This method can only find flaws in the material that are
                    open to the surface, therefore it is a surface inspection method only. Areas to be inspected must be
                    cleaned and free of paint, Insulation, or any other debris. Liquid penetrant testing is an acceptable
                    substitution for this method.
                    TEMA Recommendations:
                    In addition to any Code and contractual requirements, magnetic particle testing shall be performed
                    on all completed structural attachment welds, i.e,, lifting devices, vertical lug supports, and
                    associated reinforcement pads, as well as all locations of removed temporary attachments.




Eleventh Edition              ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                       3-7
www.tema.org       GENERAL FABRICATION AND PERFORMANCE INFORMATION                                           SECTION 3

            G-2.3.5 LIQUID PENETRANT TESTING (PT)
                    This method is used to detect surface imperfections such as cracks, surface porosity, seams, laps,
                    etc, in both magnetic and non magnetic materials and welds. A brightly colored liquid dye known as
                    a penetrant is applied to the dry and clean surface that is being examined. The penetrant is
                    generally applied by dipping, spraying, or brushing. The penetrant is left on the surface for a
                    prerequisite amount of time (known as dwell time) to allow the penetrant to be absorbed into any
                    imperfection by capillary action. Once sufficient time has passed, the excess penetrant is typically
                    removed with a solvent or water spray, and a developer is applied to the surface. The developer
                    then draws any penetrant out of the flaws so that the imperfection is revealed. This inspection is
                    performed under either ultraviolet or white light depending upon what type of dye is used-
                    Liquid penetrant testing offers a quick low-cost method for the detection of surface imperfections in
                    both non magnetic and magnetic base metal and welds. The method is simple to learn, and results
                    are very repeatable and reliable. A wide variety of part shapes and sizes can be tested. Liquid
                    penetrant is a surface inspection and will not indicate imperfections that are below the surface. The
                    irregular surface of welds may cause Talse" indications. This method can be messy or require
                    space for lighting control or drainage. Il also cannot be used on high temperature parts.
                    TEMA Recommendations:
                    In addition to any Code and contractual requirements, liquid penetrant testing shall be performed
                    on all tube end welds after expansion.
            G-2,3.6 POSITIVE MATERIAL IDENTIFICATION (PMI)
                    Positive material identification is a program that helps to ensure that the specified material is being
                    used in the construction of the exchanger. The program may make use of any combination of
                    material test reports, metallographic analyzers, and laboratory testing. It is important for each
                    manufacturer to verify that the alloying elements listed on the material test reports are verified
                    against the contractual and code requirements for Ihe specified materials.
                    Further verification on the alloying elements may be accomplished by using metallographic
                    analyzers:
                    X-ray Fluorescence (XRF): This method uses an analyzer that contains a low radioactive source
                    (isotope) or an x-ray tube, The tested material reflects the radiation from the analyzer which
                    generates energy. Each alloying element has its own atomic structure and will generate a different
                    energy level for each element. The analyzer detects and measures the reflected energy thus
                    id entityIng the alloying elements. This method does not allow for the sampling of some elements
                    such as carbon.
                    Optical Emission Spectrometry (OES): This method utilizes an analyzer with a probe which
                    releases a spark that vaporizes a small sample of the material being analyzed. The vapor contains
                    atoms and ions that produce a spectrum which can be optically measured to determine the alloying
                    components of the material. This method can be used to determine elements such as carbon.
                    Although the OES method is considered a non-destructive test in industry, the spark leaves a small
                    burn mark that will need to be removed. This method is not recommended for tubes or final gasket
                    surfaces.
                    Laser Induced Breakdown Spectroscopy (LIBS): This method uses an analyzer which emits a short
                    laser pulse to create a micro-plasma on the tested surface. The atoms and ions in the plasma emit
                    light with discrete spectral peaks as they cool. The emitted light from the plasma is collected and
                    coupled with an ICCD/spectrograph detector module for LIBS spectral analysis. Since each
                    element has a unique spectral peak, it is possible to determine the chemical makeup of the
                    sampled material by analysis of each spectral peak.
                    Material verification can also be performed through chemical analysis of a sample of the material
                    tested by a qualified laboratory. This method requires the removal of a material sample and repair
                    of the part from which the sample is removed.
                    When required, positive material identification would typically be applied to alloy pressure
                    components, fasteners, pads with alloy cladding, alloy weld consumables, and alloy welds. Some
                    services may warrant alloy verification of carbon steel components.
                    TEMA Recommendations:
                    PMI testing should be considered for alloy material.



Eleventh Edition              ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                        3-0
www.tema.org       GENERAL FABRICATION AND PERFORMANCE INFORMATION                                           SECTION 3

            G-2.3.7 FERRITE TESTING
                    Some customers may find it desirable to measure the amount of ferrite in austenitic and duplex
                    stainless steel welds and weld overlay in their equipment. Ferrite is one of the metallurgical phases
                    found in the microstructures of stainless steels. The amount of ferrite present will greatly affect the
                    mechanical properties. the weldability, and the corrosion resistance of these steels. The ferrite
                    measurement may be accomplished by either a laboratory analysis or by the use of a magnetic
                    instrument (ferritescope)
                    A laboratory analysis is a destructive test which involves the sectioning of a test specimen, and
                    performing a point count of the ferrite structures on a grid placed on photomicrographs of the
                    sectioned piece. This method would generally only be used for weld procedure qualifications.
                    The most common method for the determination of ferrite in materials and welds is by the use of a
                    magnetic instrument This method is non-destructive and can be performed on production welds
                    (except for tube-to-tubesheet welds). During this testing a probe is placed in contact with the
                    material or weld being tested which forms a closed loop. This allows the measurement of the
                    magnetic permeability of the area being tested. This permeability is measured against standard
                    percentages of other materials with known ferrite content. The magnetic permeability of the tested
                    material relative to the known standards will indicate its ferrite percentage.
                    TELIA Recommendations:
                    When welding using procedures qualified per the code of construction, ferrite testing is not normally
                    necessary nor recommended.
            G-2.3.8 BUBBLE LEAK TEST
                    Bubble leak testing can be used to reveal leak paths in welded joints Typically, this test is used for
                    welded tube-to-tubesheet joints where the bundle is placed in either its shell or a temporary test
                    shell so that the shell side of the bundle can be pressurized with air. A typical test pressure range is
                    10-1 5 psig. Once the shell side is pressurized, a soap water solution is applied to the tube side face
                    of the tubesheet. A leak in a tube joint will be indicated by causing bubbles to form. This method
                    can also be applied when testing the attachment welds for reinforcing pads that are equipped with
                    weep holes. If pressurizing the part cannot be achieved, bubble leak testing can be performed by
                    using various shaped vacuum boxes with similar results.
                    TELIA Recommendations:
                    Unless helium leak testing is performed, bubble leak testing shall be performed on tube end welds,
                    prior to Lube expansion. If the shell side cannot be pressurized or tube ends are no L accessible for
                    testing, this test is not required.
            G-2.3.9 HELIUM LEAK TEST
                    A helium leak test may be used to determine if leak paths are present in tube-to-tubesheet welds.
                    Like the bubble leak test above, this test works best when the bundle is installed in either a shell or
                    test shell so that the shell side of the bundle may be pressurized with helium. A helium mass
                    spectrometer is then used on the tube side face to detect helium amounts that are above normal
                    background levels.
                    Due to the small size of the helium atom, very small leaks can be found with this method. Helium is
                    also non-toxic and chemically inert. Helium leak testing is more costly than an air soap test due to
                    the specialized equipment required. Some configurations may not be accessible to test with this
                    method or may require a test ring such as '8 " type floating tubesheets (see E-4.1.3(3)).
                    TELIA Recommendations:
                    For critical applications, helium leak testing shall be performed on tube end welds, prior to tube
                    expansion. If the shell side cannot be pressurized or tube ends are not accessible for testing, this
                    test is not required.
            G-2.3.10 EDDY CURRENT TESTING (ECT)
                    Eddy current testing is a procedure commonly used to inspect heat exchanger and boiler tubing,
                    but it can be used in other applications. This method uses the principals of electromagnetic
                    induction to detect flaws or wall loss in tubing, either before or after the tubes are installed in an
                    exchanger. In the probe or bobbin-type testing method, the instrument is calibrated to a tube with
                    known flaws. A probe is then passed through the inside of each exchanger tube, along its full
                    length Electric current in the probe creates a magnetic field in the tube or part to be tested. The


Eleventh Edition               ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                        3-9
www.tema.org       GENERAL FABRICATION AND PERFORMANCE INFORMATION                                             SECTION 3

                     impedance of the coil within the probe changes as it interacts with the magnetic field of the part.
                     Variations in the probe impedance are measured by the instrument and shown on the instrument's
                     screen. A skilled technician interprets the signals to identify flaws or wall loss in the tubing. In the
                     encircling coil method, tubes are passed through a Stationary coil to produce similar results This
                     technique is often used at tubing mills to inspect new tubing.
                     Baseline probe-type eddy current testing is often performed on a new heat exchanger so that
                     results can be compared to later in-service tests to determine if changes have occurred after some
                     time in service. Eddy current testing is an efficient, accurate and economical method of finding
                     weakened Or damaged tubes before failure occurs. A disadvantage Of this method is the
                     specialized training needed to operate eddy current equipment and interpret testing results. Pin
                     holes and some other tube discontinuities may not be detected by this method. Only non-magnetic
                     tube materials can be tested, and fouled tubes cannot be inspected,
                     TENA Recommendations:
                     If future in-service eddy current inspections will be performed, baseline eddy current testing should
                     be considered for new units after tubes are installed in the bundle. This test is for information and
                     should not be used for rejection. This requirement should be specified by the end user
            G-2.3H INTERNAL ROTARY INSPECTION SYSTEM (IRIS TESTING)
                  This test method also uses an internal probe to inspect tubes, but in this case the probe head uses
                  a spinning mirror to perform ultrasonic inspection of the tube wall. As the probe is pulled through
                  the tube, the rotating mirror results in a helical scan path. The tube is flooded with water while
                  testing, to provide a couplant for the ultrasonic beam. This method accurately measures tube
                  thickness or any wall loss at the ID or OD and can be used on any tubing material. Limitations
                  include the high cost of equipment and operator training, and the requirements for clean tubes and
                  constant supply of water. Circumferential cracks or pin holes may not be detected.
                  TEMA Recommendations:
                  This method can be used as an alternative to eddy current testing, when dictated by tubing material
                  or other conditions.
            G-2.3.12 FLUX LEAKAGE TESTING (MFL)
                     Ferromagnetic tubes or pipes can be inspected by the magnetic flux leakage method. A probe
                     containing a magnet and flux leakage sensors is passed through the length of the Lube. Similar to
                     magnetic particle testing, flaws or defects in the parts being tested produce changes in the
                     magnetic field These changes are detected by the probe and displayed for the operator.
                     TEMA Recommendations:
                     This method can be used as an alternative to eddy current testing to monitor ferromagnetic tubes
                     for corrosion or flaws. It is not recommended unless specified by the end user
            G-2.3.13 VISUAL INSPECTION (VT)
                     As the name implies, visual testing is performed by direct, careful observation of the part being
                     tested Any suspected flaws can be investigated further by other methods such as dye penetrant
                     testing. Although visual testing is fast and can be performed on any visible part or weld, it is a
                     surface inspection only and not as accurate as other NDE types. Adequate illumination is required
                     for VT, and optical instruments such as magnifying glasses, mirrors, boroscopes, etc. may be used
                     to improve the inspection.
                     TEMA Recommendations:
                     TEMA recommends all exchangers be 100% visually inspected after fabrication is complete and
                     before hydrostatic testing. The inspection should include a full dimensional verification.
       G-2.4 SHOP OPERATION
             The detailed methods of shop operation are left to the discretion of the manufacturer in conformity with
             these Standards.

G-3 NAMEPLATES
       G-3.1 MANUFACTURER'S NAMEPLATE
             A suitable manufacturer's nameplate of corrosion resistant material shall be permanently attached to the
             head end or the shell of each TEMA exchanger, The nameplate may be attached via a bracket welded to
             the exchanger and shall be visible outside any insulation.
             G-3.1.1 NAMEPLATE DATA
                     In addition to all data required by the Code, a nameplate shall also include the following (if
                     provided):
                     User’s equipment identification
                     User’s order number
             G-3 JJ SUPPLEMENTAL INFORMATION
                    The manufacturer shall supply supplemental information where it is pertinent to the operation or
                    testing of the exchanger. This would include information pertaining to differential design and test
                    pressure conditions, restrictions on operating conditions for fixed tubesheet type exchangers, or
                    other restrictive conditions applicable to the design and/or operation of the unit or its components.
                    Such information can be noted on the nameplate or on a supplemental plate attached to the
                    exchanger at the nameplate location.
       G-3.2 PURCHASER'S NAMEPLATE
             Purchaser’s nameplates, when used, are to be supplied by the purchaser and supplement rather than
             replace the manufacturer's nameplate,
G-4 DRAWINGS AND CODE DATA REPORTS
       G-4.1 DRAWINGS FOR APPROVAL AND CHANGE
             The manufacturer shall submit an outline drawing containing information necessary for the customer to
             locate piping to the exchanger and footings or structure necessary to support the exchanger. The outline
             shall be submitted for the customer’s approval and shall show the following information as a minimum:
             nozzles sizes and locations, flange ratings for nozzles, overall dimensions, support locations and base plate
             dimensions, and exchanger weight. Other drawings may be furnished as agreed upon by the purchaser and
             the manufacturer. The drawing will be submitted electronically, in PDF format, unless another format is
             agreed upon by the purchaser and the manufacturer. It is anticipated that a reasonable number of minor
             changes may be required due to customer comments to this initial submittal. Any changes that cause
             additional expense are chargeable to the customer and it is the manufacturer s responsibility to advise the
             customer of the commercial impact. Purchaser's approval of drawings does not relieve the manufacturer of
             responsibility for compliance with this Standard and applicable Code requirements. The manufacturer shall
             not make any changes on the approved drawings without express agreement of the purchaser.
       G-4.2 DRAWINGS FOR RECORD
             After approval of drawings, the manufacturer shall furnish drawings for record. The drawings will be
             submitted electronically, in PDF format, unless another format is agreed upon by the purchaserand the
             manufacturer.
       G-4.3 PROPRIETARY RIGHTS TO DRAWINGS
             The drawings and the design indicated by them are to be considered the property of the manufacturer and
             are not to be used or reproduced without permission, except by the purchaser for internal use.
       G-4.4 CODE DATA REPORTS
             After completion of fabrication and inspection of an exchanger to its Code, the manufacturer shall furnish
             copies of the Code Manufacturer’s Data Report or Certification, as agreed upon by the purchaser and the
             manufacturer.

G-5 GUARANTEES
       G-5.1 GENERAL
             The specific terms of the guarantees should be agreed upon by the manufacturer and purchaser. Unless
             otherwise agreed upon by the manufacturer and purchaser in writing, the following paragraphs in this
             section will be applicable and will govern even over the contrary terms of any other writing between the
             manufacturer and the purchaser unless that writing specifically states that it is intended to override the
             provisions of this section.
       G-5.2 PERFORMANCE
             The purchaser shall, in writing, furnish the manufacturer with all information needed for clear understanding
             of performance requirements, including any special requirements. The manufacturer shall guarantee
             thermal performance and mechanical design of a heat exchanger, when operated at the design conditions
             specified by the purchase order or shown on the exchanger specification sheet furnished by the
             manufacturer (Figure G-1). This guarantee shall extend for a period of twelve (12) months after shipping
             date Notwithstanding this guarantee, the manufacturer shall have no responsibility or liability for excessive
             fouling of the apparatus by material such as coke, silt, scale, or any foreign substance that may be
             deposited, and the manufacturer shall have no responsibility or liability for any other performance problem
             wholly or partially caused by circumstances beyond the manufacturer's complete control or that the
             manufacturer did not have the ability to prevent. Without limiting the generality of the foregoing, such
             circumstances shall include (i) faulty installation of the exchanger by anyone Other than the manufacturer,
             (ii) any modification or repair made to the exchanger by the purchaser or anyone other than the
             manufacturer and (Hi) combination of the exchanger with other equipment not furnished by the
             manufacturer. The thermal guarantee shall not be applicable to exchangers where the thermal performance
             rating was made by anyone other than the manufacturer.
             G-5.2.1 THERMAL PERFORMANCE TEST
                     A performance test shall be made if it is established after operation for a sufficient period of lime
                     that the performance of the exchanger does not meet the written performance requirements
                     previously furnished by the purchaser to the manufacturer, provided the thermal performance rating
                     was made by the manufacturer Test conditions and procedures shall be selected by agreement
                     between the purchaser and the manufacturer to permit extrapolation of the test results to the
                     specified design conditions.
             G-5.2.2 DEFECTIVE PARTS
                     The manufacturer shall repair or replace F O B. its plant any parts proven defective within the
                     guarantee period but does not assume liability for the cost of removing defective parts or
                     reinstalling replacement parts. The manufacturer shall be responsible only for the direct costs
                     associated with repair of its defect or non-con forming product. Finished materials and accessories
                     purchased from other manufacturers, including tubes, are warranted only to the extent of the
                     original manufacturer's warranty to the heat exchanger fabricator. The manufacturer will endeavor
                     to provide the purchaser with a copy of any warranty information given to the manufacturer by
                     suppliers of parts incorporated into the exchanger by the manufacturer but cannot be responsible
                     for the accuracy or completeness of that information.
       G-5.3 DAMAGES EXCLUSION
             In no event shall the manufacturer be held liable for any indirect, special, incidental, punitive, exemplary or
             consequential damages, such as damages for loss of goodwill, work stoppage, lost profits, lost revenue,
             loss of clients, lost business or lost opportunity, or any other similar damages of any and every nature,
             under any theory of liability, whether in contract, tart, strict liability, or any other theory.
       G-5.4 CORROSION AND VIBRATION
             The manufacturer assumes no responsibility for deterioration of any part or parts of the equipment due to
             corrosion, erosion, flow induced tube vibration, or any other causes, regardless of when such deterioration
             occurs after leaving the manufacturer’s premises, except as provided for in Paragraphs G-5.2 and G-5 2 2
       G-5.5 REPLACEMENT AND SPARE PARTS
             When replacement or spare tube bundles, shells, or other parts are purchased, the manufacturer
             guarantees satisfactory fit of such parts only if it was the original manufacturer. Parts fabricated to drawings
             furnished by the purchaser shall be guaranteed to meet the dimensions and tolerances specified.

       G-5.6 DISCLAIMER OF WARRANTY
             While the manufacturer provides guarantees as specifically offered by the manufacturer to the purchaser in
             writing, the manufacturer makes no other warranties or guarantees and assumes no liability in connection
             with any other warranty or guarantee, express or implied. WITHOUT LIMITING THE GENERALITY OF THE
             FOREGOING, THE MANUFACTURER SPECIFICALLY DISCLAIMS ANY IMPLIED WARRANTY OF
             MERCHANTABILITY OR FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE IN CONNECTION WITH THE SALE
             OF EXCHANGERS, WHETHER OR NOT THE MANUFACTURER HAS BEEN ADVISED OF SUCH
             PURPOSE.
       G-5.7 AGGREGATE LIABILITY
             The aggregate total liability of manufacturer to customer for any direct loss, cost, claim, or damages of any
             kind related to any failure of performance by a heat exchanger shall not exceed the amount the purchaser
             has paid to the manufacturer for the excha nger.
       G-S.8 INDEMNIFICATION
             Notwithstanding any other contract language to the contrary, the manufacturer shall have no liability to
             indemnify, defend or hold the purchaser harmless against third-party claims, costs, losses and expenses
             relating in any way to the transaction between the manufacturer and the purchaser or to heat exchanger
             performance.


G-6 PREPARATION OF HEAT EXCHANGERS FOR SHIPMENT
       G-G.1 CLEANING
             Internal and external surfaces are to be free from loose scale and other foreign material that is readily
             removable by hand or power brushing.
       G-6.2 DRAINING
             Water, oil. or other liquids used far cleaning or hydrostatic testing are to be drained from all units before
             shipment This is not to imply that the units must be completely dry.
       G-6.3 FLANGE PROTECTION
             All exposed machined contact surfaces shall be coated with a removable rust preventative and protected
             against mechanical damage by suitable covers.
       G-6.4 THREADED CONNECTION PROTECTION
             All threaded connections are to be suitably plugged.
       G-G.5 DAMAGE PROTECTION
             The exchanger and any spare parts are to be suitably protected to prevent damage during shipment.
       G-G.6 EXPANSION JOINT PROTECTION
             External thin -walled expansion bellows shall be equipped with a protective cover which does not restrain
             movement.




Eleventh Edition                ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                           3-13
www.tema.org       GENERAL FABRICATION AND PERFORMANCE INFORMATION                                             SECTION 3

G-7 GENERAL CONSTRUCTION FEATURES OF TEMA STANDARD HEAT EXCHANGERS
       G-7.1 SUPPORTS
             All heat exchangers are to be provided with supports. The supports should be designed to accommodate
             the weight of the unit and contents, including the flooded weight during hydrostatic test.
            For purposes of support design, forces from external nozzle loadings, wind and seismic events are
            assumed to be negligible unless the purchaser specifically details the requirements. When these additional
            loads and forces are required to be considered, they need not be assumed to occur simultaneously unless
            combinations are specifically defined.
            The references under Paragraph G-7.1.3 may be used for calculating resulting stresses in the support
            structure and attachment. Acceptable methods for horizontal supports and vertical lugs are shown in the
            RGP section.
             *G-7dd HORIZONTAL UNITS
                     For units with removable tube bundles, supports should be designed to withstand a pulling force
                     equal to 1-1/2 times the weight of the tube bundle.
                     Horizontal units are normally provided with at least two saddle type supports, with holes for anchor
                     bolts. The holes in all but one of the supports are to be elongated to accommodate axial movement
                     of the unit under operating conditions. Other types of support may be used if all design criteria are
                     met, and axial movement is accommodated.
             *G-7X2 VERTICAL UNITS
                   Vertical units are to be provided with supports adequate to meet design requirements. The supports
                   may be of the lug, annular ring, leg or skirt type. If the unit is to be located in a supporting structure,
                   the supports should be of sufficient size to allow clearance for the body flanges.
            G-7.1.3 REFERENCES
