A. 3 SPECIAL CASES
            Special consideration must be given to tubesheet designs with abnormal conditions of support or loading.
            Following are some typical examples:
            (1) Exchangers with large differences in shell and head inside diameters; e g. fixed tubesheets with kettle
                 type shell
            (2) The adequacy of the staying action of the tubes during hydrostatic test; e.g., with test rings for types S
                 and T, or types P and W.
            (3) Vertical exchangers where weight and/or pressure drop loadings produce significant effects relative to
                  the design pressures.
            (4) Extreme interpass temperature differentials.
            Consideration may also be given to special design configurations and/or methods of analysis which may
            justify reduction of the tubesheet thickness requirements.




Eleventh Edition              ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                      A-20
www.tema.org                               REPAIRS AND ALTERATIONS                                        APPENDIX B

           NON-MANDATORY APPENDIX B - REPAIRS AND ALTERATIONS OF HEAT EXCHANGERS
     The following information has been provided to aid designers and end users in planning for and executing repairs
     and/or alterations of shell and tube heat exchangers or their components.
B.1 PLANNING REPAIRS AND ALTERATIONS
     Repairs or alterations to heat exchangers may be required or desired for many reasons, including damaged or
     leaking pressure containing components, changes in service, or modifications to nozzles, supports, or exchanger
     configurations. In some cases, the most cost-effective option may be to replace the unit or component entirely In
     deciding whether to repair or replace a damaged exchanger or component, the following should be considered:
         (1 ) The overall condition of the exchanger. If certain components require repair or rework but the remainder of
              the unit is in good condition, repairs may be warranted.
         (2) Material and construction lead times for new equipment.
         (3) Cost considerations - repairs are generally less expensive than new equipment.
         (4) Feasibility of repairs - repairs to some items may not be possible.
         (5) Expected service life - new units could last much longer than repaired units.
       B,1, 1 DETERMINING THE SCOPE OF A REPAIR OR ALTERATION

             The scope of repair or alteration may be determined by inspections, testing results, exchanger performance
             changes, or planned changes to unit designs or configuration. The scope may change during the repair
             process if additional damaged components are found.
             Some factors to consider when determining the scope include:
             (1) Timeframe for nepair/aIteration
                   (a) If the equipment is to be installed as part of scheduled plant maintenance, when does the reworked
                       equipment need to be on site?
                   (b) How soon can the equipment be taken out of service?
                   (c) Are facilities available for cleaning the equipment after removal from service?
                   (d) What NDE will need to be performed to determine if the equipment is fit for service after repair or
                       alteration?
                   (e) Can the repair/alteration facility support the scope of work for the reworked equipment?
             (2) Material considerations
                   (a) If metallurgy of any components is altered, the design of the equipment will need to be evaluated
                       for code compliance and possibly thermal performance
                   (b) Does material availability and lead time support the project schedule?
                   (c) Older or corroded material may be difficult to weld repair.
                   (d) Remaining heat treat cycles should be considered for low-alloy equipment.
             (3) Repair location and access
                       Repairs can often be made on site if access allows. However, repairs made in a shop are usually
                       less expensive.
       B.1.2 CONSULT WITH THE ORIGINAL EQUIPMENT MANUFACTURER (OEM)
             Whenever possible, the original equipment manufacturer should be consulted prior to any work that is
             performed on equipment they supplied. The OEM is the most knowledgeable with regards to their
             equipment, design philosophies, and manufacturing processes used. The OEM is the most reliable source
             for help concerning repair and alteration of their equipment and should be contacted to maintain any
             equipment warranty.




Eleventh Edition                 ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                    B-1
www.tema.org                            REPAIRS AND ALTERATIONS                                            APPENDIX B

       B.1.3 REPAIR PLAN AND CODE/JURISDICTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
            Any repair or alteration should only be made after a careful review of any special requirements of the local
            jurisdiction in which the equipment is operated. This may include design requirements, NDEr stress relief,
            stamping, and/or inspection. Repairs and alterations of ASME Code stamped equipment are usually
            performed in accordance with the National Board Inspection Code (NBIG), In addition, all requirements of
            either the original or the current code of construction should be met. It should be noted that use of the
            current code of construction may force changes which are not desirable in a replacement part of the
            exchanger. A detailed repair/alteration plan should be prepared which describes the scope of the repair or
            alteration and should include (when available or applicable):
            (1) Repair Drawing
            (2) Original Equipment Drawing and U-1 Data Report
            (3) Repair or Alteration Design Calculations
            (4) Required NDE
            (5) Stress Relieving
            (6) Code Stamping
            (7) Code Documentation
            Prior to commencing any repair or alteration, the Jurisdiction representative for the location where the
            equipment is in operation and the fabricator's Authorized Inspector should be consulted as to the extent of
            the repair and the proposed repair plan.
       B.1.4 POST RECEIPT CLEANING AND INSPECTION
            Exchangers are exposed to many types of fluids with varying levels of toxicity and combustibility It is
            important that all heat exchanger components are cleaned so they are safe to work upon. This is
            particularly necessary prior to any 'hot work' such as welding and cutting. Cleaning of exchangers should
            be performed at a qualified facility prior to receipt at the point of repair. Cleaning methods may involve
            chemical cleaning, steam cleaning, mechanical cleaning, or abrasive blasting. MSDS reports for any
            chemicals previously in the unit should be supplied to repair contractors or receiving facilities for reference.
            It is highly recommended that photos of the unit be taken when the unit is received at the point of repair to
            document the condition of the unit prior to Start Of the repair. Markings On the unit should be verified to
            confirm that the correct unit was received. The unit should be visually inspected for wasted areas, cracks,
            damaged gasket surfaces, or any other damaged areas. Further NDE may be necessary should visual
            inspection reveal areas of concern. The overall condition of the unit should be determined to confirm it is fit
            to be placed in service following the repair or alteration Any areas of concern that could change the scope
            of repair should be brought to the attention of the owner by the inspecting facility and agreed upon by all
            parties concerned.




Eleventh Edition               ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                        B-2
www.tema.org                              REPAIRS AND ALTERATIONS                                            APPENDIX B

B.2 REPAIR OF HEAT EXCHANGER COMPONENTS
     The cause of any defect, whether in a weld or in base material, should be determined (if possible) in order to
     prevent future defects of the same nature The extent of the defect should also be established to determine if repair
     of the component is practical or if the component should be replaced.
             ROUTINE AND MAJOR REPAIRS.
             (1) ROUTINE REPAIRS
                       Routine repairs often do not require inspection by the Authorized Inspector and may not require
                       stamping of the repaired component. Routine repairs do not require the component to be post weld
                       heat treated. A routine repair may not require any NDE beyond visual inspection under the original
                       code of construction,
             (2) MAJOR REPAIRS
                       More extensive repairs are not considered routine and require engagement of an Authorized
                       Inspector. They also may require post weld heat treatment additional NDE (radiography, ultrasonic
                       inspection, etc.) and repair stamping.
             (3) SOME EXAMPLES OF REPAIRS (Routine and Major)
                   (a) Weld repairs of wasted areas either in welds or in base materials.
                   (b) Welded attachment of non -pressure components such as pass partition plates, lugs, clips, anchors,
                       brackets, etc. to pressure components.
                   (c) Weld overlay of corrosion resistant (high alloy and nonferrous) materials, where the overlay is not
                       being considered for strength in design.
                   (d) Weld repairing or replacing nozzles (same size/wall/materials) in shells and channels.
                   (e) Rewelding of tube to tubesheet joints.
                   (f) Replacing tubes (retubing).
                   (g) Plugging leaking tubes.
                   (h) Restoring flange gasket surfaces.
       B.2.2 RETUBING OF AN EXISTING BUNDLE AND BUNDLE REPLACEMENT
             (1) RETUBING OF AN EXISTING BUNDLE
                       Replacing the tubes in a bundle may be considered when it is desirable to save the expense of the
                       tubesheet and/or baffle material and the machining/drilling of these parts. This option will typically
                       require the unit to be down for a longer period of time compared to a removable bundle
                       replacement, since the replacement bundle can be fabricated and supplied to the plant site prior to
                       the unit going down for maintenance. Fora retube, the unit must be taken down prior to removal of
                       the bundle and remain down while the bundle is being retubed. There is also a risk that the
                       tubesheet and or baffles may be deemed unfit for service once the bundle has been removed and
                       inspected. Fixed tubesheet exchangers can be retubed, but bundle replacement is usually not a
                       cost-effective option.
                       Some factors to consider when retubing a bundle include:
                         (a) Compare the expense of potentially replacing the tubesheet and/or baffles and the extra
                             down time required to retube the bundle to the cost of a new replacement bundle.
                         (b) Availability of shop space and labor to retube the bundle in a timely manner.
                         (c) Lead limes of tubing and other materials support the retube of the bundle.
                         (d) Availability of bundle cleaning facilities so that it can be safely retubed.
                         (e) Condition of tubesheet(s) and baffles/baffle assembly.
                         (f) Do the tube holes in the tubes heet(s)/baffles meet TEMA tolerances? (see RCB-7.2)
                         (g) Will the tube material and thickness change due to obsolete material or upgrades?
                         (h) Note: Tube material and thickness are integral in the calculation of the tubesheet thickness.
                             Changes in these elements can affect the tubesheet thickness. The required tubesheet
                             thickness must be confirmed if the tube material or thickness is changed. (See Alteration
                             below if tube material or thickness is changed)



Eleventh Edition                 ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                       B-3
www.tema.org                            REPAIRS AND ALTERATIONS                                          APPENDIX B

            (2) BUNDLE REPLACEMENT
                    For removable bundle configurations, bundle replacement may be preferable to retubing for several
                    reasons:
                      (a) The bundle components will be new and should provide a long service life.
                      (b) The bundle will be on hand prior to the unit being taken down for maintenance thus
                          minimizing downtime.
                      (c) If a change in metallurgy is desired, the bundle can be properly designed to meet Code
                          requirements for the new metallurgy.
                      (d) Bundle design can be modified to address existing issues (vibration, acoustics, pressure
                          drop, etc.)
       B.2.3 REPAIRING TUBE TO TUBESHEET JOINTS (SEE RCB-T3}
            It is often desirable to repair tube to tubesheet joints in existing bundles. Expanded-only joints may relax
            due to service, thermal cycling, transport, or handling Welded joints may be compromised due to erosion or
            corrosion during service and require rewelding.
            (1) Expanded tube to tubesheet joints may need to be re-expanded if they have lost their seal over time.
                The re-expansion may be accomplished by mechanical, pneumatic, or hydraulic roll equipment Tubes
                may also be expanded by explosive or hydraulic methods Since lubes are plastically deformed during
                the initial expansion process, it will be necessary to exceed the wall reduction of original expansion
                when rerolling Care should be given to control the amount of plastic deformation of the tubes during
                the re-expansion It is recommended that the additional expansion be done by roll equipment
                Reworked tube to tubesheet joints should be subjected to a pressure or leak test to verify that a
                successful seal has been achieved.
            (2) Welded tube to tubesheet joints may be rewelded- Care should be exercised to ensure removal of all
                defects or contamination that would hinder the rewelding of the tube joint. Liquid penetrant testing
                should be used to detect any weld cracks, undercuts, or pin holes. Tube projection, weld preparation,
                and any required preheat should be established prior to welding to ensure the proper tube to tubesheet
                joint can be achieved. It is recommended that any additional expansion be performed by roll expansion
                equipment. Pressure testing is also recommended for rewelded lube to tubesheet joints to verify a
                successful seal has been achieved.
       B.2.4 PLUGGING LEAKING TUBES. (SEE E-4.8 AND RGP-RCB-2)
            Tubes may be plugged when it is not practical to replace them, such as the inner rows of a U-tube bundle.
            Care should be given to properly identify the leaking tube or tubes (see E-4.1.3). Once the tube is identified
            it should have holes cut in it to provide for future venting and draining of the plugged tube. If either end of
            the tube is loose, it should be secured so that it does not damage the adjacent tubes. The tube ends or tube
            holes in the tubesheet may be plugged by tapered tube plugs driven into the tube ends or tubesheet holes.
            The plugs may also be secured by welding if preferred. Tube plug metallurgy shall be compatible with the
            tube material. See E-4.8 concerning liabilities resulting from plugging of tubes.
       B.2.5 RESTORING FLANGE GASKET SURFACES
            Flange gasket surfaces may be restored to the correct finish by re-ma chining. Where necessary, weld metal
            build up may be applied to wasted surfaces prior to machining in order to meet the required flange
            thickness. Weld metal build up or overlay must be compatible with the flange material or corrosion resistant
            weld overlay where applicable. Proper NDE techniques, preheat, and post weld heat treatment should be
            applied as necessary to ensure the weld metal build up or overlay has a good bond, is of adequate
            thickness, and has proper chemistry.




Eleventh Edition               ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                      B-4
www.tema.org                            REPAIRS AND ALTERATIONS                                           APPENDIX B

B.3 RERATING AND ALTERATION OF HEAT EXCHANGER COMPONENTS
            RERATING HEAT EXCHANGER COMPONENTS
            If the exchanger design can support higher pressure and/or temperature ratings than what is stamped on
            the nameplate, the exchanger can be re-rated to the higher conditions. Current thickness readings, visual
            inspection, and a pressure test for all pressure boundary components on the side being rerated may be
            required.
            Note: It is sometimes necessary to replace heat exchanger components to satisfy the new required design
            conditions (see E-5).
            For any new service, fixed tubesheet type exchangers must also have the new mean metal temperatures
            and the need for an expansion joint evaluated.
       B.3.2 ALTERATION OF HEAT EXCHANGER COMPONENTS
            It is sometimes desirable or necessary to change certain aspects of a heat exchanger (See E-5), This is
            considered an alteration and may include but not limited to:
            (1) Changing materials of construction on pressure boundary components
            (2) Changing exchanger type or configuration (See N-1.2)
            (3) Changing the number of tube side passes
            (4) Changing nozzle sizes
            (5) Changing the quantity, diameter, or wall thickness of tubes
       B.3.3 REPURPOSING OF A HEAT EXCHANGER OR ITS COMPONENTS
            A heat exchanger is considered repurposed when it is used in a service other than that for which it was
            originally designed (See E-5). Care should be exercised when placing an exchanger into a different service
            (1) A metallurgist should be consulted to verify the materials of the exchanger are suitable for the process
                fluids.
            (2) All applicable operating and upset conditions must not exceed the design temperatures and maximum
                allowable pressures of the equipment. Fixed tubesheet type exchangers must also have the new mean
                metal temperatures and the need for an expansion joint evaluated.
            (3) A thorough inspection of the equipment should also be performed to insure it is in satisfactory condition.
            (4) Are any modifications of the equipment required prior to placing the exchanger in service?
B.4 TESTING AND DOCUMENTATION
       B.4.1 TESTING AND NONDESTRUCTIVE EVALUATION {NDE)
            Testing requirements for repairs and alterations should be in accordance with either the original or the
            current code of construction whenever possible. Testing of repairs and alterations should be approved by
            the Authorized Inspector and Jurisdictional authorities when applicable (see G-2.3). NDE methods may
            include but are not limited to:
            (1) Visual Inspection
            (2) Magnetic Particle Examination
            (3) Liquid Penetrant Examination
            (4) Radiographic Examination
            (5) Ultrasonic Examination
            (6) Positive Material Examination
            (7) Pressure Testing
            (8) Leak Testing
       B.4.2 DOCUMENTATION REQUIREMENTS
             The following documentation should be provided for repairs and alterations, when applicable:
            (1) The As-Built repair plan or drawing, showing all required details of the re pair/aIteration.
            (2) NDE certificates or reports of any testing performed.
            (3) The proper repair/aIteration reports or forms as required by the local jurisdiction or authorizing agency.
            (4) Any partial data reports supplied for replacement components as required by the local jurisdiction or
                authorizing agency.
            (5) Material Test Reports for materials used, if applicable.



Eleventh Edition               ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                      B-5
www.tema.org                       CLAD AND OVERLAY CONSTRUCTION                                           APPENDIX C

          NON-MANDATORY APPENDIX C - GUIDELINES FOR CLAD AND OVERLAY CONSTRUCTION
     The following information has been provided as general guidelines for clad and weld overlay construction for shell
     and tube heat exchangers and heat exchanger components. Other methods of corrosion protection are available,
     such as intermittently bonded liners, but only integrally bonded methods are discussed here.
C-1 CLAD AND OVERLAY CONSIDERATIONS
     Heat exchangers are often exposed to corrosive services where high alloys such as stainless steels, non-ferrous
     alloys, Or titanium are preferred to mitigate the corrosive effects of the fluids and provide dependable service These
     alloys are more costly when compared to carbon and low alloy steels. Carbon and low alloy steels have the added
     benefit that their allowable stresses are generally higher than those of the high alloy materials which result in more
     economical designs. A good alternative to solid high alloy construction is to clad or weld overlay the interior of a
     carbon or low alloy heat exchanger shell or channel with a corrosion resistant lining of high alloy material. This
     provides the benefit of the higher strength of the carbon or low alloy base material and the improved corrosion
     resistance of the high alloy lining. This type of construction is more labor intensive since it often involves a more
     complex manufacturing process and additional noncestruetive examination (NDE), For smaller, lower pressure,
     lower temperature designs, the solid high alloy construction may prove to be more economical
     Clad and overlay construction will also require a longer fabrication cycle. There are few suppliers of clad materials;
     therefore, the delivery of the clad materials can extend construction of the heat exchanger. Clad and overlay
     construction is a time-consuming process. The actual fabrication of the exchanger is often interrupted to perform
     necessary in-process NDE.
     Types of integrally bonded clad and overlay construction:
         (1) Roll bonded cladding - This type of clad is made from a sheet or plate of high alloy material that is placed
             on a plate of carbon or low alloy base material. The two plates are heated to a high temperature and run
             through rolls which places great pressure on the two plates to bond them together. The starting thickness
             of both the clad and base material is carefully chosen to provide the desired finished thickness after the
             completion of the bonding process. This process is generally done at a mill. Since high temperature is
             required to achieve this bond, not all alloys can be bonded in this manner. This process works for plate.
             Roll bonding is not recommended for tubesheets.
         (2) Explosion bonded cladding - This type of clad is made from a sheet or plate of high alloy material that is
             offset a specific distance from a plate or forging of carbon or low alloy base material. An explosive charge
             is evenly distributed over the clad material. When the charge is detonated, the explosion moves across the
             clad material and bonds the two plates together. The edges of the composite plate are then trimmed, and
             the plate is flattened as necessary. The starting thickness of both the clad and base material is carefully
             chosen to provide the desired finished thickness after completion of the bonding process. A wide range of
             materials can be bonded in this manner.
         (3) Weld overlay and back cladding - Weld overlay is a versatile method to apply a corrosion resistant high
             alloy layer to carbon or low alloy base material. Weld overlay can be applied by many combinations of
             welding processes, and weld consumables are selected to achieve the desired chemistry at the specified
             depth for the weld overlay. Weld overlay is normally applied in two or more weld passes to achieve the
             desired thickness for the high alloy material Weld overlay can be applied to plate, pipe, or forgings The
             thickness of the weld overlay is normally greater than that of clad materials. The difference in these
             thicknesses needs to be considered in the design of the equipment and is discussed in the next section.
             Back cladding is used to restore the corrosion resistant material that has been removed at weld seams.
             Weld overlay is not recommended with some materials such as titanium.




Eleventh Edition                ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                      C-1
www.tema.org                      CLAD AND OVERLAY CONSTRUCTION                                          APPENDIX C

C-2 DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS
     Corrosion resistant alloy clad and weld overlay are not considered as pressure retaining unless this is allowed by
     the purchaser and all code requirements to permit this have been satisfied- When calculating the thickness of the
     pressure retaining components only the base metal should be considered. The inside diameter of pressure retaining
     parts used in the calculations shall be at the interfacial surface between the base metal and cladding/weld overlay.
     When base metal is excavated to allow for thicker weld overlay/clad restoration, the greater diameter at the
     excavated base metal shall be used in the calculations (see Figure C-2). For components that will not be excavated
     into the base material the diameter of the base material shall be used for the calculations. Cladding thickness shall
     not be considered as contributing to the strength of the flange. The cladding and weld overlay shall cover the gasket
     surface of the flange, tubesheet or cover Any weld overlay shall be added to the flat face thickness of nozzle
     flanges. Standard flanges (see Table D-3) shall not be excavated below the specified thickness of the flange to
     provide for the addition of weId overlay.
                                                      FIGURE C-2




                   WELD PREPARATION
                   PER MANUFACTURER




              CLAD CYL. LONGITUDINAL                               CLAD CYL. TO GIRTH FLANGE
          AND CIRCUMFERENTIAL SEAMS

                                                       BLIND




                                         WELD OVERLAID LWN NOZZLE
                                             AND BLIND FLANGE




Eleventh Edition               ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                     C-2
www.tema.org                      CLAD AND OVERLAY CONSTRUCTION                                          APPENDIX C

C-3 NON-DESTRUCTIVE EXAMINATION
     Any n on-destructive examination should be in accordance with the applicable code of construction and customer
     Specifications. Since clad and weld overlaid exchangers are often in aggressive or severe services, proper NDE
     should be observed to ensure that the exchanger will perform correctly and provide dependable service for its
     expected life. The following NDE may be considered. See Section G-2.3 Non-Destructive Testing Techniques for
     more information concerning NDE.
       C-3.1 CHEMICAL SOLUTION EXAMINATION
             Some of the alloying elements in corrosion resistant cladding or weld overlay can adversely affect the
             pressure retaining welds of the base metal and must be removed from the area near the weld bevels in the
             base materials prior to welding of these pressure retaining joints. Applying a specialized chemical solution
             such as copper sulfate, ferroxyl, or copper ferricyanide to these areas will reveal if the cladding or weld
             overlay has been adequately removed.
             Examine all areas where cladding or weld overlay have been removed for welding of base materials.
       C-3. 2 LIQUID PENETRANT (PT) EXAMINATION
             (1) PT areas where cladding or weld overlay has been stripped back prior to application of clad restoration.
             (2) PT final layer of weld overlay and clad restoration. If the component is subject to post weld heat
                 treatment, this examination shall be performed after the post weld heat treatment
             (3) PT areas of cladding and weld overlay where non-pressure parts, such as pass partition plates, lugs, or
                 brackets, will be attached.
             (4) PT overlaid gasket surfaces after final machining.
       C-3.3 ULTRASONIC (UT) EXAMINATION
             Ultrasonic examination can be used to check clad or overlaid materials to reveal if there are areas of
             disbondment between the cladding and the base material. Ultrasonic examination can also be used to
             determine the thickness of the deposited weld overlay.
             UT clad/overlaid plate from the clad/overlaid surface in areas where non-pressure parts, such as pass
             partition plates, lugs, or brackets, will be attached.
       C-3,4 FERRITE
             Ferrite testing requirements shall be agreed upon with the purchaser.
       C-3.5 PMI / ALLOY VERIFICATION
             PMI and alloy verification methods, testing depth, sampling frequency, and acceptable ranges shall be
             agreed upon with the purchaser.

             GENERAL INSTRUCTIONS FOR INSTALLATION, OPERATION, AND MAINTENANCE OF
                                        SHELL AND TUBE HEAT EXCHANGERS


                                               SAFETY CONSIDERATIONS
LIFTING DEVICES

     When eyebolts, lugs, clips or other devices are installed by the manufacturer, it is intended that these lifting devices
     are to lift only the empty components to which they are attached and not the assembled heat exchanger, unless
     explicitly specified otherwise.

     Proper rigging must be used in lifting assembled heat exchangers. Exchangers are usually much heavier than
     pressure vessels of comparable dimensions; therefore, weights should be carefully evaluated. When available,
     determinations should include reference to manufacturer’s drawings, added components, bill of lading, etc. Special
     care must be exercised in handling exchangers with expansion joints. Metal loss of lifting devices and surrounding
     areas shall be considered prior to their use, especially when attachment is to cylinders or heads where the
     predominant metal loss is not visible.


ROUTINE PRECAUTIONARY PROCEDURES

     A heat exchanger is a multi-chamber pressure vessel designed at specific limits of pressure, temperature and fluid
     flow conditions as shown on the exchanger specification sheet and heat exchanger nameplate(s). The process
     system, which includes the heat exchanger, must be safeguarded so that the heat exchanger design conditions and
     operating limits are not exceeded. All operating and maintenance personnel should be made aware of specific
     limitations including pressures and temperatures, flow rates, start-up/shut-down procedures, and cleaning
     procedures

     Do not remove channel covers, shell covers, floating head covers, bonnets, or connecting piping until all pressure
     has been relieved and both shell and tube sides are completely drained. Exceptions are permissible when design
     permits independent pressure testing of shell and tube sides.

     Plugged tubes and double tubesheet connecting chambers, unless vented, may remain pressurized after shell and
     tube sides are depressurized, Caution should be exercised in loosening tube plugs or opening vents to avoid
     sudden release of pressure or harmful fluids.

     Many heat exchangers circulate fluids which are toxic, lethal, flammable, or dangerous to personnel. These fluids
     could cause problems if bolted or threaded joints are not maintained in leak-tight condition under operating or no¬
     flow ambient conditions. Proper precautions, such as effective draining and purging, must be taken in handling and
     decontamination when exchangers are opened for any reason, It is essential that the user advise maintenance or
     repair personnel, including outside contractors, when toxic, lethal, or flammable conditions exist Users should
     provide Material Safety Data Sheets for any fluids that may remain in the exchanger

     When heat exchangers are cleaned, it is important that full characteristics of the circulating fluids, fouling material,
     and the cleaning agent be known and care exercised in handling them. Use eye protection, a respirator, or other
     appropriate protective devices.

     Do not blow out heat exchangers with air when the process fluids or the cleaning fluids being handled are
     flammable or reactive

                     SECTION 2 HEAT EXCHANGER IDENTIFICATION AND DEFINITIONS
       IOM-2.1 EXCHANGER IDENTIFICATION
             IOM-2.1,1 COMPLETE EXCHANGER
                   Manufacturers normally assign a serial number which is unique and permanently identifies each
                   exchanger. Inquiries to the manufacturer should reference this number when it is shown on the
                   nameplate and manufacturer's drawings. Additional useful information normally shown includes
                   size, type, and item number
             IOM-2.1, 2 EXCHANGER PARTS
                   Exchanger parts should be identified by the exchanger serial number and by the name and part
                   number corresponding to those shown in Table IOM-1 2, or as shown on the manufacturers
                   drawings.
       IOM-2.2 DEFINITIONS
             IOM-2.2,1 EXCHANGER UNIT
                   One or more exchangers designed for a specific service.
             IOM-2.2, 2 ITEM NUMBER
                   The purchaser’s identification number for an exchanger unit.
             IOM-2.2, 3 NORMAL OPERATING CONDITIONS
                   The thermal and hydrostatic performance requirements generally specified for designing the heat
                   exchanger.
             IOM-2.2. 4 UPSET CONDITIONS
                   A departure from specified operating conditions.
             IOM-2, 2.5 PULSATING FLUID CONDITIONS
                   Conditions of flow generally characterized by rapid fluctuations in pressure and flow rate resulting
                   from sources outside of the heat exchanger such as: pumps, compressors, blowers, etc.
             IOM-2.2.6 START-UP CONDITIONS
                   The conditions of operation which exist from the time that flow of either or both process streams is
                   initiated to the time that steady-state operating conditions are achieved.
             IOM-2.2. 7 SHUT-DOWN CONDITIONS
                   The conditions of operation which exist from the time of steady-state operating conditions to the
                   time that flow of both process streams has ceased.




Eleventh Edition               ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                      D-9
www.tema.org                                          I0M MANUAL                                             APPENDIX D

                                  SECTION 3 INSTALLATION OF HEAT EXCHANGERS
IOM-3 INSTALLATION
       IOM-3.1 INSTALLATION PLANNING
            IOM-3.1.1 CLEARANCE FOR DISMANTLING
                   For straight tube exchangers fitted with removable bundles, provide sufficient clearance at the
                   stationary head end to permit removal of the bundle from the shell and provide adequate space
                   beyond the rear head end to permit removal of the shell cover and/or floating head cover.
                   For fixed tubesheet exchangers, provide sufficient clearance at one end to permit withdrawal and
                   replacement of the tubes and enough space beyond the head at the opposite end to permit removal
                   of the bonnet or channel cover.
                   For U-Tube heat exchangers, provide sufficient clearance at the stationary head end to permit
                   withdrawal of the tube bundle, or at the opposite end to permit removal of the shell.
            IOM-3.1.2 FOUNDATIONS
                       Foundations must be adequate so that exchangers will not settle and impose excessive strains on
                       the exchanger. Foundation bolts should be set to allow for setting inaccuracies. In concrete
                       footings, pipe sleeves at least one size larger than the bolt diameter slipped over the bolt and cast
                       in place are best for this purpose, as they allow the bolt center to be adjusted after the foundation
                       has set.
            IOM-3.1.3 PIPING
                   IOM-3.1.3.1 THERMAL EXPANSION
                            It is essential that adequate provisions be made in the connecting piping to allow for thermal
                            expansion without imposing unspecified piping loads on the exchanger. This is particularly
                            important for single pass internal floating head designs, outside packed floating heads, and
                            fixed tubesheet types with shell expansion joints. Unless precautions are taken, leakage or
                            damage may result
                   IOM-3.1.3.2 PULSATION AND VIBRATION
                            In all installations, care must be taken to eliminate transmission of fluid pulsations and
                            mechanical vibrations to the heat exchangers.
            IOM-3.1.4 CLEANING
                       Convenient means should be provided for cleaning the unit as suggested under "Maintenance.”
                       (Section 5)
             IOM-3.1.5 LEVELING
                       Exchangers must be installed so that pipe connections are made without forcing, disassembling, or
                       loosening of flanged assemblies.
            IOM-3.1,6 STACKED HEAT EXCHANGERS
                       The manufacturer normally stacks exchangers prior to the completion of fabrication to insure proper
                       alignment of interconnecting nozzles. Shims separating the intermediate shell supports are usually
                       used. Therefore, it is important that any shims used match the thickness and locations used by the
                       manufacturer.
             IOM-3.1.7 CONNECTION PROTECTORS
                       Remove shipping coversand plugs immediatelly prior to installation. Before connecting to piping,
                       inspect all openings in the heat exchanger for foreign material. The entire system should be clean
                       and free of foreign objects before starting operation. Do not expose heat exchanger internals to
                       moisture or harmful contaminants.
            IOM-3.1.8 PIPE CONNECTIONS
                       Pipe connections must be in accordance with the manufacturer's dimensional outline drawing.
            IOM-3.1.9 FOUNDATION BOLTS
                       Foundation bolts should be loosened at one end of the unit to allow free expansion of shells,
                       Slotted holes in supports are provided for this purpose.


Eleventh Edition                  ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                     D-10
www.tema.org                                      IOM MANUAL                                            APPENDIX D

            IOM-3.1.10 BELLOWS TYPE EXPANSION JOINT
                    If the heat exchanger is equipped with a bellows type expansion joint, the expansion joint must be
                    adequately protected during shipping and setting Shipping supports must be removed prior to
                    operation in accordance with manufacturer's instructions.
       IOM-3.2 HEAT EXCHANGER STORAGE
            Heat exchangers are not normally protected for storage. If storage is necessary, a covered location at
            uniform temperature is preferable and provisions must be made to protect the heat exchanger interior from
            moisture, freezing, or harmful contaminants,
       IOM-3.3 RECOMMENDATIONS FOR RETIGHTENING
            Heat exchangers are pressure tested before leaving the manufacturer's shop. However, normal relaxation
            of gasket joints may occur in the interval between testing in the manufacturer’s shop and installation at the
            job site. Therefore, all external bolted joints should be checked for lightness by leak testing or torque
            verification after installation and, if necessary, after the exchanger has reached operating temperature. See
            IOM-5 8 for bolt tightening procedure-




Eleventh Edition              ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                    D-11
www.tema.org                                      I0M MANUAL                                             APPENDIX D

                                SECTION 4 OPERATION OF HEAT EXCHANGERS
IOM-4 OPERATION
       10M-4.1 PERFORMANCE OF HEAT EXCHANGERS
            Satisfactory performance and service life can be expected only from heat exchangers which are properly
            installed, operated in accordance with design conditions, and receive preventive maintenance and cleaning
            on an appropriate schedule. These requirements are the responsibility of the user.
            The failure of heat exchanger equipment to perform satisfactorily may be caused by one or more factors,
            such as:
            (1) Excessive fouling.
            (2) Air or gas binding resulting from improper piping installation or lack of suitable vents.
            (3) Operating conditions differing from design conditions.
            (4) Maldistribution of flow in the unit.
            (5) Excessive clearances between the baffles and shell and/or tubes, due to corrosion,
            (6)    Improper thermal design.
            (7) Flooding resulting from inadequate drainage of condensate.
            (8) Material selection.
       IOM-4.2 OPERATION OF HEAT EXCHANGERS
            IOM-4, 2,1 OPERATING PROCEDURES - NORMAL
                    Before placing any exchanger in operation, reference should be made to the exchanger drawings,
                    specification sheet(s), and nameplate(s) for any special instructions. Local safety and health
                    regulations must be considered. Improper start-up or shut-down sequences, particularly of fixed
                    tubesheet units, may cause leaking of tube-to-tubesheet and/or bolted flanged joints.
            IOM-4. 2.2 START-UP OPERATION
                    Most exchangers with removable tube bundles may be placed in service by first establishing
                    circulation of the cold medium, followed by the gradual introduction of the hot medium. During start¬
                    up all vent valves should be opened and left open until all passages have been purged of air and
                    are completely filled with fluid. For fixed tubesheet exchangers, fluids must be introduced in a
                    manner to minimize differential expansion between the shell and tubes (refer to IOM-4.2. 4).
            IOM-4,2.3 SHUT-DOWN OPERATION
                    For exchangers with removable bundles, the units may be shut down by first gradually stopping the
                    flow of the hot medium and then stopping the flow of the cold medium. If it is necessary to stop the
                    flow of the cold medium, the circulation of the hot medium through the exchanger should also be
                    stopped For fixed tubesheet exchangers, the unit must be Shut down in a manner to minimize
                    differential expansion between shell and tubes (refer to IOM-4. 2. 4). When shutting down the
                    system, all units should be drained completely when there is the possibility of freezing or corrosion
                    damage. To guard against water hammer, condensate should be drained from steam heaters and
                    similar apparatus during start-up or shutdown. To reduce the water retention after drainage, the
                    tube side of water-cooled exchangers should be blown out with air.
            IOM-4.2.4 START-UP AND SHUT-DOWN PROCEDURES FOR FIXED TUBESHEET EXCHANGERS
                    When start-up and shut-down instructions are furnished, they must be followed. When specific
                    instructions are not supplied, the recommended procedure is to start, or stop, the circulation of both
                    shell and tube side fluids gradually at the same time




Eleventh Edition              ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                     D-12
www.tema.org                                         IOM MANUAL                                              APPENDIX D

       IOM-4. 3 OPERATING LIMITATIONS
            IOM-4.3,1 NORMAL OPERATING CONDITIONS
                       Equipment must not be operated al conditions more severe than those specified on the heat
                       exchanger specification sheet and/or nameplates. A change in service or deviation from the
                       specified operating conditions may require that a mechanical design check be made in order to
                       avoid possible damage to the heat exchanger.
            IOM-4.3.2 SUPPLEMENTAL NAMEPLATE DATA
                       The manufacturer shall supply supplemental information where it Is pertinent to the Operation or
                       testing of the exchanger. This would include information pertaining to differential design and test
                       pressure conditions, restrictions on operating conditions for fixed tubesheet type exchangers, or
                       other restrictive conditions applicable to the design and/or operation of the unit or its components.
                       Such information can be noted on the nameplate or on a supplemental plate attached to the
                       exchanger at the nameplate location.
             IOM-4.3.3 FLOWRATE
                       Heat exchangers are not to be operated at flow rates greater than those shown on the heat
                       exchanger specification sheet. Excessive flow rates can cause vibration and/or erosion and
                       severely damage the heat exchanger.
            IOM-4.3.4 HYDRAULIC HAMMER
                       Exchangers should not be subjected to pulsating, slugging, or unstable flow conditions which may
                       impair performance or result in destructive hydraulic hammer. Adequate condensate drainage is
                       essential for steam heaters and similar apparatus.
            IOM-4.3. 5 TEMPERATURE SHOCK
                       Exchangers normally should not be subjected to abrupt temperature fluctuations. Hot fluid must not
                       be suddenly introduced when the unit is cold, nor cold fluid when the unit is hot.
            IOM-4.3. & FLUID TEMPERATURE LIMITS
                   IOM-4.3.6.1
                       Heat exchangers are not to be operated at fluid temperatures more severe than those shown on
                       the heat exchanger specification sheet
                   IOM-4.3.6. 2
                       For fixed tubesheet exchangers, fluid inlet temperatures may be severely limited when there is no
                       fluid on the opposite side. Operation with fluid circulation on one side only should be avoided,
                       unless specifically provided for in the design of the heat exchanger.




Eleventh Edition                  ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                     D-13
www.tema.org                                       IOM MANUAL                                              APPENDIX D

                              SECTION 5 MAINTENANCE OF HEAT EXCHANGERS
IOM-5 MAINTENANCE
       IOM-5.1 INSPECTION OF UNIT
            At regular intervals and as frequently as experience indicates, an examination should be made of the
            interior and exterior condition of the unit. Neglect in keeping all tubes clean may result in complete
            stoppage of flow through some tubes which could cause severe thermal strains, leaking lube joints, or
            structural damage to other components. Sacrificial anodes, when provided, should be inspected to
            determine whether they should be cleaned or replaced.
            IOM-5.1.1 INDICATIONS OF FOULING
                    Exchangers subject to fouling or scaling should be cleaned periodically. A light sludge or scale
                    coating on lubes greatly reduces Iheir efficiency. A marked increase in pressure drop and/or
                    reduction in performance usually indicates cleaning is necessary. The unit should first be checked
                    for air or vapor binding to confirm that this is not the cause for the reduction in performance. Since
                    the difficulty Of cleaning increases rapidly as the scale thickness Or deposit increases, the intervals
                    between cleanings should not be excessive.
            IOM-5.1. 2 PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE
                   Additional important reasons for inspections include:
                   (1 ) Corrosion and erosion may weaken various parts of an exchanger and may eventually cause
                        mechanical failure or impaired performance.
                   (2) Determination of fouling and corrosion rales for prediction of service life and scheduling down¬
                        time.
                   (3) Implementation of preventive maintenance procedures which reduce rates of fouling and
                        general deterioration or eliminate leaks.
            IOM-5.1. 3 ACCESS TO TUBES
                    Before disassembly, the user must verify that the unit has been depressurized, vented and drained,
                    neutralized and/or purged of hazardous materials.
                    To inspect the inside of the tubes and also make them accessible for cleaning, the following
                    procedures should be used:
                    (1) Stationary Head End
                        (a)   Type A, C, D, and N: remove channel cover only.
                        (b)   Type B? remove bonnet.
                    (2) Rear Head End
                        (a)  Type L, N, and P: remove channel cover only,
                        (b)  Type M, remove bonnet
                        (c) Type S and T; remove shell cover and floating head cover.
                        (d) Type W, remove channel cover or bonnet.




Eleventh Edition              ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                       D-14
www.tema.org                                      IOM MANUAL                                              APPENDIX D

            IOM-5.1.4 LOCATING TUBE LEAKS
                   The following procedures may be used to locate perforated or split tubes and leaking joints
                   between tubes and tubesheets. In most cases, the entire front face of each tubesheet will be
                   accessible for inspection. The point where water escapes indicates a defective tube or tube-to-
                   tubesheet joint.
                   (1) Units with removable channel cover: Remove channel cover and apply hydrostatic pressure in
                       the shell.
                   (2) Units with bonnet type head: For fixed tubesheet units where tubesheets are an integral part of
                       the sheik remove bonnet and apply hydrostatic pressure in the shell. For fixed tubesheet units
                       where tubesheets are not an integral part of the shell and for units with removable bundles,
                       remove bonnet, re-bolt tubesheet to shell or install test flange or gland, whichever is applicable,
                       and apply hydrostatic pressure in the shell. See Figure
                       IQM-5 1,4-1 for examples of some typical test flanges and test glands.
                                                        FIGURE IOM-5. 1.4-1




                   (3) Units with Type S Or T floating head: Remove channel cover or bonnet shell cover and floating
                       head cover. Install test ring and bolt in place with gasket and packing. Apply hydrostatic
                       pressure in the shell. A typical test ring is shown in Figure IOM-5.1.4-2. When a test ring is not
                       available it is possible to locate leaks in the floating head end by removing the shell cover and
                       applying hydrostatic pressure in the tubes. Leaking tube joints may then be located by sighting
                       through the tube lanes. Care must be exercised when testing partially assembled exchangers
                       to prevent over extension of expansion joints or overloading of tubes and/or tube-to-tubesheet
                       joints.
                   (4) Metal temperature during hydrostatic test shall be at least GOT (16°C} or as permitted by the
                      applicable code.




Eleventh Edition             ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                       D-15
www.tema.org                                       I0M MANUAL                                               APPENDIX D

                                                          FIGURE IOM-5 1.4-2




       IOM-5.2 TUBE BUNDLE REMOVAL
            Bundles mu st be withdrawn from the stationary end as noted on the drawing. All parts such as glands,
            retainer rings, split shear rings and closure packing must be removed before attempting to remove the
            bundle. Threaded eyebolts may be attached to the stationary tubesheet when tapped holes have been
            provided for this purpose. When eyebolt tappings are not provided, rods may be passed through two or
            more tubes and attached to a steel bearing plate over the floating tubesheet Provide a protective spacer
            between the bearing plate and tubesheet to protect the tube ends.
            Tube bundles should not be handled with hooks or other devices which might damage the tubes. Bundles
            should be supported on cradles or skids. Horizontal tube bundles should be lifted by means of suitable
            slings. Care should be taken to avoid bending or damaging baffles during bundle removal. All gasket and
            packing contact surfaces should be protected from accidental damage since these areas are generally
            difficult to repair.
       IOM-5.3 CLEANING TUBE BUNDLES
            IOM-5.3.1 CLEANING METHODS
                    The heat transfer surfaces of heat exchangers should be kept reasonably clean to assure
                    satisfactory performance. Convenient means for cleaning should be made available.
                    Heat exchangers may be cleaned by either chemical or mechanical methods. The method selected
                    must be the choice of the operator of the plant and will depend on the type of deposit and the
                    facilities available in the plant. Following are several cleaning procedures that may be considered:
                    (1 ) Circulating hot wash oil or light distillate through tubes or shell at high velocity may effectively
                         remove sludge or similar soft deposits.
                    (2) Some salt deposits may be washed out by circulating hot fresh water.
                    (3) Commercial cleaning compounds are available for removing sludge or scale provided hot wash
                         oil or water is not available or does not give satisfactory results.
                    (4) High pressure waterjet cleaning.
                    (5) Scrapers, rotating wire brushes, and other mechanical means for removing hard scale, coke, or
                         other deposits.
                    (6) Employ services of a qualified organization that provides cleaning services. These
                         organizations will check the nature of the deposits to be removed, furnish proper solvents

Eleventh Edition              ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                        D-16
www.tema.org                                        I0M MANUAL                                           APPENDIX D

                         and/or acid solutions containing inhibitors, and provide equipment and personnel fora complete
                         cleaning job.
            IOM-5.3.2 CLEANING PRECAUTIONS
                     (1) Tubes should not be cleaned by blowing steam through individual tubes since this heats the
                         tube and may result in severe expansion strain, deformation of the tube, or loosening of the
                         tube-to-tubesheet joint.
                     (2) When mechanically cleaning a tube bundle, care should be exercised to avoid damaging the
                         tubes.
                     (3) Cleaning compounds must be compatible with the metallurgy of the exchanger.
                     (4) Cleaning procedures, especially those which circulate hot fluids through the tubes, can cause
                         significant temperature differentials between the shell and tubes. Such cleaning procedures can
                         cause damage to fixed-tubesheet heat exchangers and should be avoided unless specifically
                         provided for in the design of the heat exchanger.
       I0M-S.4 GASKET REPLACEMENT
            Gasket and gasket surfaces should be thoroughly cleaned and should be free of scratches and other
            defects Gaskets should be properly positioned before attempting to retighten bolts It is recommended that
            when a heat exchanger is dismantled for any cause, it be reassembled with new gaskets. This will reduce
            the likelihood of future leaks and/or damage to the gasket seating surfaces of the heat exchanger.
            Composition gaskets become dried out and brittle so that they do not always provide an effective seal when
            reused. Metal or metal jacketed gaskets, when compressed initially, flow to match their contact surfaces. In
            doing so they are work hardened and, if reused, may provide an imperfect seal or result in deformation and
            damage to the gasket contact surfaces of the exchanger. Graphite faced gaskets are usually damaged in
            disassembly and as such cannot be reused. Any leakage at a gasketed joint should be rectified and not
            permitted to persist as it may result in damage to the gasket surfaces. See IOM-4.4 for other
            recommendations.
            Bolted joints and flanges are designed for use with the particular type of gasket specified. Substitution of a
            gasket of different construction or improper dimensions may result in leakage and damage to gasket
            surfaces Therefore, the bolted joint shall be evaluated for any gasket substitution by the original
            manufacturer or other competent designer.
            When metal jacketed filled type gaskets are used with a tongue and groove joint without a nubbin, the
            gasket should be installed so that the tongue bears on the seamless side of the gasket jacket. When a
            nubbin is used, the nubbin should bear on the seamless side.
       IOM-5.5 SPARE AND REPLACEMENT PARTS
             The procurement of spare or replacement parts from the manufacturer will be facilitated if the correct name
             for the part, as shown in IOM-1, Table IOM-1, of this manual is given, together with the serial number, type,
             size, and other information from the nameplate. Replacement parts should be purchased from the original
            manufacturer,

       IOM-5.6 EXPANDED TUBE JOINTS
            IOM-5.6.1 TUBE HOLE FINISH
                   Tube hole finish affects the mechanical strength and leak tightness of an expanded tube-to-
                   tubesheet joint. In general:
                   (1 ) A rough tube hole provides more mechanical strength than a smooth tube hole. This is
                        influenced by a complex relationship of modulus of elasticity, yield strength, and hardness of
                        the materials being used.
                   (2) A smooth tube hole does not provide the mechanical strength that a rough tube hole does, but
                        it can provide a pressure tight joint at a lower level of tube wall reduction.
                   (3) Very light wall tubes require a smoother tube hole finish than heavier wall tubes.
                   (4) Significant longitudinal scratches can provide leak paths through an expanded tube-to-
                        tubesheet joint and should therefore be removed.




Eleventh Edition               ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                    D-17
www.tema.org                                        IOM MANUAL                                              APPENDIX D

            IOM-5.6.2 TUBE WALL REDUCTION
                     The optimum tube wall reduction for an expanded tube-to-tubesheet joint depends on a number of
                     factors, some of which are:
                     (1) Tube bole finish,
                     (2) The presence or absence of the tube hole serrations (grooves).
                     (3) The tube hole size and tolerance.
                     (4) Tubesheet ligament width and its relation to tube diameter and thickness.
                     (5) Tube wall thickness.
                     (6) Tube hardness and change in hardness during cold weather.
                     (7) Tube OD tolerance.
                     (8) Type of expander used.
                     (9)   Type of torque control or final tube thickness control.
                     (10) Function of tube joint, i.e., strength in resistance to pulling out, minimum cold work for
                           corrosion purposes, freedom from leaks, ease of replacement, etc.
                     (11) Length of expanded joint.
                     (12) Compatibility of tube and tubesheet materials.
            IOM-5.6.3 TUBE EXPANDING
                     A suitable tube expander should be used to tighten a leaking tube joint Care should betaken to
                     ensure that tubes are not over expanded. Do not re-expand tubes which are not leaking. Do not
                     expand tubes within 1/8" (3 mm) from the back face of the tubesheet.

            IOM-5.6.4 PLUGGING TUBES IN TUBE BUNDLES
                     Tubes may be plugged when it is not practical to replace them, such as the inner rows of a U4ube
                     bundle. Care should be given to properly identify the leaking tube or tubes. Once the tube is
                     identified it should have holes out in it to provide for future venting and draining of the plugged tube.
                     If either end of the lube is loose, Il should be secured so that it does not damage the adjacent
                     tubes. The tube ends or tube holes in the tubesheet may be plugged by tapered tube plugs driven
                     into the tube ends or tubesheet holes. The plugs may also be secured by welding if preferred. Tube
                     plug metallurgy shall be compatible with the tube material. Excessive tube plugging may result in
                     reduced thermal performance, higher pressure drop, and/or mechanical damage. It is the user's
                     responsibility to remove plugs and neutralize the bundle prior to sending it to a shop for repairs.
       IOM-5.7 WELDED TUBE JOINTS
            Welding procedure and testing techniques for either seaI -welded or strength- welded tube joints normally
            are by agreement between manufacturer and purchaser and therefore should be considered prior to repairs
            to welded tube joints or tube replacements
       IOM-5,8 RECOMMENDED BOLT TIGHTENING PROCEDURE
            IOM-5.8.1
                     All gasket joint surfaces shall be clean and free of oil or debris. If the gasket requires assistance to
                     be held in place for installation , grease shall not be used. Any tape applied to a spiral wound
                     gasket for shipping or assembly shall be removed prior to installing the gasket- No tape, string, or
                     other object will be allowed to remain on the gasket surface once assembly is complete. ASME
                     PCC-1 Section 6 provides additional guidance for the installation of gaskets.
            IOM-5.8.2
                    Thoroughly clean threads, nut faces and the flange where nut face bears. If roughness, burrs or
                    any irregularity is present, dress it out to as smooth a surface as possible, or replace bolting.
             IOM-5.8.3
                    Thoroughly lubricate threads on studs, nuts and contacting surfaces on nuts and flange. ASME
                    PCC-1 Section 7 provides additional guidance for the lubrication of fasteners.
             IOM-5.8.4
                    The joint shall be snugged up squarely so the entire flange face bears uniformly on the gasket.
                    ASME PCC-1 Section 5 and Appendix E provide additional guidance for the alignment of joints.




Eleventh Edition               ©Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association, Inc.                                        D-18
www.tema.org                                         I0M MANUAL                                           APPENDIX D

            IOM-5.8.5
                     Tightening of the bolts shall be applied in at least three equally spaced increments using a cross¬
                     bolting pattern as illustrated in Figure IOM-5.8 ora pattern as recommended by ASME PCC-1
                     Sections 8 through 11 .
            IOM-5.8.6
                   When the cross-bolting pattern is used and is complete; a circular chase pattern shall be applied
                     until no nut rotation occurs.
                                                     FIGURE IOM-5.8
                                                            START




             The following may be used as references and aids when assembling belted flanged joints:
            1. ASME PCC-1 , Guidelines for Pressure Boundary Bolted Flange Joint Assembly.
            2. WRC Bulletin 538, Determination of Pressure Boundary Joint Assembly Bolt Loads, 2nd Edition
            3. An Introduction to the Design and Behavior of Bolted Joints, by John H. Bickford
            4. ASME Section VIII, Division I, Appendix S.
       IOM-5.9 SPECIAL HIGH-PRESSURE CLOSURES
            The instructions of the heat exchanger manufacturer should be followed when provided.
       IOM-5.10 REPAIRS, ALTERATION, RERATING, AND REPLACEMENT PARTS
            To maintain the Code integrity of the vessel, any repairs by welding, subsequent heat treating, alterations,
            replacement pressure parts, or rerating must be done in accordance with the local jurisdiction and Code.
            Consideration of the original design may be required-
             Potentially toxic or harmful contaminants must be neutralized prior to repairs.
             Following repair by welding, post weld heat treatment may be a requirement for preservation of resistance
             to corrosion
            See TEMA Appendix B for further information.
